title: Double Play Depth: Position and Execute

root.narration: Runner on first, nobody out, and your team leads by two. You're playing second base. Before the pitch, your coach yells 'Double play depth!' Time to think about where to stand and how to set up for the twin killing.

decision-1.narration: Your coach says 'double play depth!' How do you position yourself as the second baseman?

choices.text: Move in a few steps toward home and shade toward second base. Need to be close enough to turn two!
Stay at my normal depth. I can still turn two from here.
Play deep to take away hits through the middle. Range is more important.

transition-hit.narration: You move in two steps and shade a step toward second base. Perfect double play depth! The pitcher delivers and the batter smacks a sharp grounder to your LEFT -- toward the hole between you and first base. You dive and snag it! But you're on the ground now. What's the play?

decision-2.narration: You've got the ball after a diving stop! You're on the ground at second base. The shortstop is covering the bag at second. The runner from first is coming hard. What do you do?

choices.text: Flip the ball to the shortstop at second base from the ground -- underhand toss!
Crawl to second base yourself and step on it for the force!
Forget second -- throw to first from the ground to get the batter!

decision-3.narration: Nice flip! The shortstop catches it right at the bag -- FORCE OUT at second! One out! The shortstop now has the ball and needs to complete the double play. The batter is about two-thirds of the way to first. The runner from first is sliding in hard at the shortstop's legs. How does the shortstop handle the relay?

choices.text: Jump over the sliding runner, plant, and fire to first!
Skip the jump -- just sidearm it to first as fast as possible!
The runner is coming right at me -- just hold the ball. One out is enough.

outcome-dp-great.narration: The shortstop leaps over the sliding runner like a ballet dancer, plants their back foot, and fires a bullet to first. The first baseman stretches -- GOT IT! DOUBLE PLAY! The infield erupts! 6-4-3 on the scorecard! From zero outs to two outs on one pitch. That's elite defense!

outcome.headline: 6-4-3 Double Play! Beautiful Turn!
outcome.explanation: The 6-4-3 (shortstop to second baseman to first baseman... wait, this was 4-6-3: second baseman to shortstop to first baseman!) is one of baseball's most beautiful plays. Here's what made it work: 1) You positioned at double play depth so you could reach the ball AND be close to second base. 2) You flipped to the shortstop quickly with a soft underhand toss (hard throws from close range are harder to handle). 3) The shortstop jumped to avoid the slide AND to get a clear throwing lane to first. 4) The throw beat the runner. Every step in the chain has to work perfectly. That's teamwork!
outcome.whatToRemember: The double play is a CHAIN: field cleanly, feed the pivot man softly, the pivot man avoids the runner and makes a strong throw. Every link matters. Position yourself to make the chain as short as possible.

outcome-sidearm-good.narration: The shortstop catches the feed, touches second for the force, and slings a sidearm throw to first without jumping. The throw tails a bit but the first baseman scoops it on one hop. The umpire looks... SAFE! The batter beat the throw by half a step! You got one out, but the double play didn't turn.

outcome.headline: One Out, Batter Safe. Almost Had Two!
outcome.explanation: The sidearm throw is faster to release, but it sacrifices accuracy. Because the shortstop didn't jump, the sliding runner partially blocked their throwing lane, and the throw tailed away from the first baseman. When turning a double play, the pivot man (the person at second base receiving the flip) needs to do three things: 1) catch the ball, 2) touch the bag, 3) get ABOVE the sliding runner to throw. The jump isn't just for safety -- it gives you a cleaner throwing angle to first base. Still, getting the force out at second with nobody out is a solid play.
outcome.whatToRemember: When turning a double play, the jump over the runner isn't just for show -- it gives you a clear throwing lane to first base. Without it, the sliding runner can disrupt your throw.

outcome-eat-okay.narration: The shortstop catches the flip, touches second, and holds the ball as the runner slides in. One out, but the batter reaches first. Runner on first with one out instead of nobody on with two outs. The safe play -- but you left an out on the table.

outcome.headline: One Out. Double Play Missed.
outcome.explanation: There are definitely times to eat the ball -- if the throw to first would be wild or dangerous, take the sure out. But in this case, the shortstop had time and the throw was there. Learning to turn the double play takes practice and courage. The sliding runner looks scary, but with proper footwork (the jump!), you stay safe and make the throw. In a game where you're up by two with nobody out, one out is fine. But in a closer game, that second out could be the difference between winning and losing.
outcome.whatToRemember: Take the sure out when you're uncertain, but PRACTICE the double play turn so you're confident making it in games. The more you rep it, the more natural the jump-and-throw becomes.

outcome-normal-okay.narration: You stay at your normal depth. The batter smacks a grounder toward you -- you field it, but now you're too far from second base! You have to make a long throw to the shortstop covering second. The throw is a little wide and the shortstop has to stretch for it. They get the force at second, but the batter beats the relay to first. Only one out.

outcome.headline: One Out. Too Far for the Turn.
outcome.explanation: At normal depth, you're positioned to take away more hits through the outfield grass. That's great in some situations! But with a runner on first and nobody out, the double play is your BEST weapon. By playing too deep, you made the flip to second base longer and slower, which made the whole double play chain take too long. Double play depth means moving in a few steps and shading toward second base. Yes, you give up some range, but you gain the ability to turn two -- which is worth it when you NEED a double play.
outcome.whatToRemember: Double play depth = a few steps IN and a step TOWARD second base. You sacrifice some range, but you gain the ability to turn two. Always know the situation before the pitch.

outcome-deep-bad.narration: You move BACK, playing deep. The batter chops a grounder right at you -- easy fielding. But you're so far from second base that by the time the ball gets to the shortstop, the runner from first is already there. No force at second! The shortstop has to tag the runner, who slides in safely. You throw to first and get the batter, but the runner is on second with one out. No double play possible from that deep.

outcome.headline: No Double Play! Way Too Deep!
outcome.explanation: Playing deep gave you great range, but it completely eliminated the double play. The extra distance meant: 1) Your flip to second took longer. 2) The runner from first arrived at second before the ball. 3) No force out was possible, so the shortstop would have needed to tag the runner. In a double play situation, you MUST be close enough to second base to make the flip quickly. The whole point of 'double play depth' is to trade a little range for the ability to get two outs on one play. When the game situation screams 'double play,' your positioning should too.
outcome.whatToRemember: You can't turn a double play from deep in the hole. When the situation calls for a double play, cheat toward the bag. The double play is worth more than the extra range.

outcome-tag-self-okay.narration: You scramble toward second base trying to touch it yourself, but the runner from first gets there before you do! No force out. You hold the ball and the runner is safe at second. The batter reaches first. Nobody is out and now you've got runners on first and second. Yikes!

outcome.headline: No Outs! Runner Beat You to the Bag!
outcome.explanation: When you're on the ground after a diving stop, you can't race a runner to the bag -- they have too much of a head start. That's exactly why you have a TEAMMATE covering second base! The shortstop was right there, ready to receive a flip. A soft underhand toss to the shortstop would have gotten the force out easily, and they could have turned two. Trust your teammates! Baseball is a team sport, and the double play only works when everyone does their job. Your job after the diving stop was the flip, not the crawl.
outcome.whatToRemember: After a diving stop, flip the ball to the covering fielder at the bag -- don't try to reach the base yourself. Your teammate is in a better position to make the play.

outcome-first-only-okay.narration: You throw to first from the ground and get the batter! Nice arm! But the runner from first is now standing safely on second base. One out, runner in scoring position. You got an out, but you chose the LESS important one.

outcome.headline: Out at First, Runner Advances.
outcome.explanation: You got an out, which is never terrible. But by throwing to first, you let the lead runner advance to scoring position at second base. If you had flipped to the shortstop at second first, you would have gotten the lead runner AND had a chance at the double play. Remember the principle: get the LEAD runner first. They're the dangerous one. The batter at first base is much less threatening than a runner at second base. Also, from the ground, a short flip to second is easier and more accurate than a long throw to first.
outcome.whatToRemember: Always get the lead runner when possible. A flip to second base starts the double play AND removes the more dangerous runner. Short flips beat long throws.
